item.title: Chuwi Minibook X: the netbook we deserve
item.description: Netbooks are dead, but the Chuwi Minibook X scratches the same itch.

item.title: Weird projects I shipped with AI
item.description: Where are all the AI-generated projects? This is a common question from AI skeptics: if LLMs are so good at writing code, where is the tsunami of new AI-generated apps, services and games?

item.title: PrismML — Introducing 1-bit and Ternary Bonsai Image 4B: Image Generation for Local Devices
Converting ISO Country Codes to Flag Emojis
item.description: Did you know that there's a simple formula you can use to convert from an ISO two-letter country code to the emoji of the flag of that country. I've made an interactive thingy to demonstrate it, and shared some other fun things I've learned while playing around with ISO 3166 and flag emoji.
item.author: (https://www.facebook.com/itsdanq)

item.title: The S in interoperability
item.description: The S in interoperability

item.title: Typeface Specimens of Houston Mono™
item.description:
Neil Panchal, the creator of soon-to-released Typeface Houston Mono™, recently sent me a complimentary set of the TX-24 Houston Mono typeface specimens. Some of you know this probably, but U.S Graphics is also the company that created the famous Berkeley Mono typeface.
I purchased Berkeley Mono years ago and

item.title: From Chat Completions to an Agents API – Notes by Dennis Yurkevich
item.description: A note on the shift from chat completions to agents that return finished work, and what that means for builders outside frontier labs.

item.title: Cheap software won't make engineering cheap
item.description: John Wang's personal website

item.title: Simple systems are the best systems
item.description:
When it comes to systems design, John Gall knew his stuff. He even has a LAW1 about it:
A complex system that works is invariably found to have evolved from a simple system that worked. A complex system designed from scratch never works and cannot be patched up to make it work. You have to start over with a working simple system.
Gall isn’t the only promoter of simple systems. Here’s Dijkstra (maybe you’ve heard of him?)

item.title: Colonies on Mars require more than engineering
item.description: Elon Musk talks about a city on Mars the way a developer talks about a new subdivision outside Phoenix. The engineering problems are hard, but they are the kind of hard we know how to attack: get there cheaply, make propellant on site, close the life-support loop, shield against the radiation, and eventually warm the place up. There is a technical roadmap. There is a financial roadmap. There is no social roadmap at all.

item.title: Why I'm building a SaaS company during the SaaSpocalypse.
item.description: When building is cheap, all that remains is focus, taste, and conviction.
item.author: (Vincent Chan)

item.title: Auditing DeepSWE
item.description: Last updated 2026-05-31.
item.author: (June Kim)

item.title: The Agentic Mesh: Cognitive Automation at Scale
item.description: Convictions for designing tomorrow’s agentic systems — an agent mesh system capable of delivering value at scale, borrowing principles from the data mesh.

item.title: Encrypted Client Hello: A Big Tech Privacy Fix
item.description: Much of the traffic on the web is now encrypted thanks to SSL/TLS (https). This prevents attackers from seeing the contents of your traffic. But they can still see which domains you are connecting to, and that might be a problem still.

item.title: Snapshot-based CMEK Bypass: Cross-Project Disk Replication
item.description: This follow-up demonstrates a snapshot-based bypass that complements the disk-cloning attack described earlier.

item.title: Scientists found that the creatine supplement millions take for muscle gains is quietly raising brain energy levels and slowing early Alzheimer's cognitive decline by 30% - thesciverse
item.description: Tens of millions of people take creatine every day for their muscles. A comprehensive review and a landmark clinical trial published in 2025 and 2026 have now documented what the same supplement is quietly doing to their brains. Creatine crosses the blood-brain barrier, raises phosphocreatine levels in neurons, and provides the ATP buffer that keeps cognitive performance from hitting an energy ceiling during demanding mental work. In early Alzheimer's patients, it slowed cognitive decline by approximately 30% versus placebo in a controlled trial. In healthy adults under sleep deprivation, a single dose measurably improved cognitive performance. In depression patients, adding it to cognitive behavioral therapy significantly improved outcomes beyond therapy alone. None of this is mentioned on the label.

item.title: Cloudflare Turnstile requiring fingerprintable WebGL
item.description:
Since about a week, Cloudflare Turnstile (their "Verify you're human" device verification) has been looping indefinitely in my webkit-gtk based browser. Preventing access to quite few websites (previously, but it even went worse lately).
Turns out it's because Cloudflare wants to have a fingerprint of your device via WebGL, the only reason for doing this would be tracking.

item.title: the solution might be cancelling my AI subscription
item.description: I am trying to think of a list of all the wonderful things I've built with AI:

item.title: The Website Specification
item.description: A platform-agnostic, full specification of the technical features a good website should have. Built in the open under an MIT licence.

item.title: AV2 Specification
item.description: AV2 is the next-generation video coding specification from the Alliance for Open Media (AOMedia). Building on the foundation of AV1, AV2 is engineered to provide superior compression efficiency, enabling high-quality video delivery at significantly lower bitrates. It is optimized for the evolving demands of streaming, broadcasting, and real-time video conferencing.

item.title: Shantell Sans → A font for you
item.description: A new typeface from Shantell Martin
